past_medical_history: Mitral valve prolapse diagnosed incidentally at age 25 on echocardiogram
Left tibial plateau fracture at age 22, surgically repaired with plate and screws
Mild obstructive sleep apnea diagnosed at age 28, non-compliant with CPAP

medications.indication: Seizure prophylaxis, started after second generalized seizure

family_history: Cousin with epilepsy (generalized tonic-clonic seizures since childhood, on valproate)
Father with deep vein thrombosis at age 55
Mother with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
No family history of stroke or sudden cardiac death

social_history.occupation: Commercial airline pilot, currently grounded due to seizure history and FAA medical certificate suspension
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 2-4 beers per week, reduced since seizure onset
social_history.living_situation: Lives with partner at approximately 6,500 feet elevation in Colorado
social_history.exercise: Previously active with mountain biking and hiking, has curtailed activities due to concern about seizure recurrence
social_history.travel: Completed a 20-hour car trip from Colorado to Texas two weeks before most recent presentation

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Appears mildly anxious about ongoing diagnostic uncertainty. Speech fluent without paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Attention intact with digit span 7 forward and 5 backward. Memory 3/3 registration, 3/3 recall at 5 minutes. Fund of knowledge appropriate for education level.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing intact bilaterally to finger rub. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius full strength bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone in all four extremities. Strength 5/5 throughout including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, interossei, iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings, tibialis anterior, and gastrocnemius bilaterally. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibratory sense intact in all four extremities. No dermatomal sensory loss. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal stride length and base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Mild tenderness noted over the left calf posteriorly, with no obvious swelling, warmth, or erythema on cursory examination. Homans sign equivocal. No focal neurological deficits attributable to the reported seizures.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent early-morning generalized seizures of unclear etiology with recent onset of left leg pain, exertional dyspnea, and hemoptysis
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Brennan is a 31-year-old right-handed commercial airline pilot who presents for inpatient neurological evaluation following two generalized tonic-clonic seizures over the past two months, with a confounding presentation of progressive cardiopulmonary and lower extremity symptoms that has complicated his diagnostic workup. His first seizure occurred approximately two months ago, witnessed by his partner in the early morning hours. He was found to be tonic-clonic with rhythmic jerking of all four extremities lasting approximately 90 seconds, followed by post-ictal confusion lasting 15 minutes. He denied any preceding aura, tongue biting was noted, and there was urinary incontinence. He presented to the emergency department where initial neurological evaluation was performed, and he was discharged with neurology follow-up arranged. Of note, he had experienced mild left leg pain beginning the day before this first seizure, which he attributed to a muscle strain from hiking at altitude.

Approximately one week after the first seizure, he presented to urgent care with worsening left leg pain localized to the calf and popliteal region, along with new exertional dyspnea that he noticed during routine activities. He was evaluated briefly and treated for presumed seasonal allergies exacerbating exercise-induced symptoms, without any diagnostic testing performed. He continued to experience intermittent left leg discomfort.

His second generalized seizure occurred approximately three weeks after the first, again in the early morning hours with a similar pattern of bilateral tonic-clonic activity. He was started on levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily by his neurologist at that time. Despite this, he reports continued episodes of a peculiar rising sensation in his epigastrium accompanied by a brief feeling of deja vu, occurring approximately two to three times per week, each lasting 15 to 30 seconds, after which he feels momentarily confused. He has not reported these episodes to his physician, attributing them to anxiety related to his career uncertainty.

He now presents to the emergency department with a three-day history of progressive dyspnea, a single episode of hemoptysis yesterday, worsening left calf pain radiating to the popliteal fossa, and intermittent central chest tightness. He completed a 20-hour car trip from Colorado to Texas two weeks ago for a family event. His primary concerns are the respiratory and leg symptoms, but his emergency physician has consulted neurology given the seizure history. He is admitted for a comprehensive workup. He is particularly distressed about the implications for his flying career, as the FAA requires seizure freedom for consideration of medical recertification.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz well-organized alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness achieved with vertex waves; no sustained sleep captured

findings.morphology: Arciform, monophasic waves at 6-11 Hz without aftergoing slow wave. Maximum amplitude 75 microvolts. No field consistent with epileptiform discharge.

findings.frequency: Intermittent 6-7 Hz theta range activity superimposed on normal background

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, partially obscuring temporal regions during sustained wakefulness
QRS complexes visible and could potentially be confused with sharp transients

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed. Mild symmetric build-up of background slowing, returned to baseline within 90 seconds. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving at mid-range frequencies. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. Wicket spikes identified as a benign normal variant in the left temporal region. Mild intermittent diffuse theta slowing is non-specific and may reflect medication effect or drowsiness. No definite epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures captured during this 30-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Only brief drowsiness achieved, no sustained sleep recorded. Routine 30-minute duration limits sensitivity. Muscle artifact intermittently obscured temporal regions.

findings.location: Left lingula of the lung (incidentally noted on scout images)

volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 3.4 cm3 (45th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 3.5 cm3 (48th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No evidence of mesial temporal sclerosis
No cortical dysplasia or migrational abnormality identified on standard sequences
No mass lesion or pathological enhancement
Incidental 4 mm lingular lung nodule noted on sagittal scout image; recommend dedicated chest CT for further characterization
Single periventricular white matter focus of uncertain significance; differential includes nonspecific small vessel change or migraine-related
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI without evidence of epileptogenic structural lesion. No hippocampal sclerosis, mass, cortical malformation, or vascular abnormality. Single non-specific periventricular white matter focus, likely incidental. Incidental 4 mm lingular pulmonary nodule noted on scout images requiring dedicated chest imaging for further evaluation. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with borderline sinus tachycardia. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia or conduction abnormality identified.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: D-dimer 4850 ng/mL FEU (H), Prolactin 42.6 ng/mL (H), Levetiracetam level 8.2 mcg/mL (L).

background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, and sleep spindles. All sleep stages recorded during 72-hour monitoring.

findings.frequency: Rare during wakefulness (average 1-2 per hour), increasing during NREM sleep (average 6 per hour)
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F7, duration 50-70 ms, followed by aftergoing slow wave. Clear epileptiform morphology distinct from previously reported wicket spikes.
findings.state: Predominantly NREM sleep, occasional during wakefulness

findings.location: Left anterior temporal onset (F7/T3), remaining lateralized to left hemisphere
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with left temporal rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta activity evolving over 30 seconds, followed by attenuation, then left temporal 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta. Each event lasted 25-45 seconds. No bilateral spread observed.
findings.state: Both during wakefulness, one in early morning

findings.type: Electroclinical seizure - focal to bilateral tonic-clonic
findings.location: Left anterior temporal onset (F7/T3), evolving to bilateral hemispheric involvement
findings.frequency: One event captured, occurring during early-morning NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Left temporal rhythmic theta onset evolving over 40 seconds to bilateral rhythmic delta, then bilateral tonic-clonic activity with generalized polyspike and wave. Total duration 110 seconds.
findings.state: Arising from NREM sleep (early morning, 04:47 AM)

artifacts.impact: Partially obscured mid-ictal EEG during the generalized phase, but focal onset clearly captured

output.impression: Abnormal 72-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. Three habitual seizures captured: two focal aware seizures with epigastric aura and deja vu, and one focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure arising from NREM sleep. All seizures demonstrated unequivocal left anterior temporal electrographic onset at F7/T3. Interictal left anterior temporal epileptiform discharges, maximal during sleep. The initially normal routine EEG is explained by the rarity of interictal discharges during wakefulness and the short recording duration. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Three-day monitoring; longer recording might capture additional events.

findings.location: Left mesial temporal cortex, anterior aspect of the left hippocampus and parahippocampal gyrus

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Subtle blurring of gray-white matter junction in the left anterior temporal pole
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Subtle hyperintensity in the left anterior hippocampus and adjacent cortex on thin coronal cuts (2 mm)
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Faint cortical FLAIR hyperintensity in the left temporal pole, easily missed on standard sequences

volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 3.1 cm3 (25th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 3.5 cm3 (48th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: Subtle left anterior temporal abnormality was NOT visible on the prior 1.5T standard brain MRI

The gray-white junction blurring and cortical signal change raise suspicion for focal cortical dysplasia (type I or II) or alternatively very early hippocampal sclerosis in the left anterior temporal region
Hippocampal volumetric asymmetry index is borderline, with the left hippocampus smaller than the right
The 4 mm lingular lung nodule previously noted on scout images is not re-evaluated on this brain-dedicated protocol
output.impression: 3T epilepsy protocol MRI reveals a subtle cortical abnormality in the left anterior temporal region with gray-white matter junction blurring and faint T2/FLAIR cortical hyperintensity, suspicious for focal cortical dysplasia. Left hippocampal volume is borderline reduced (asymmetry index 0.12). The left temporal location is concordant with the left temporal electrographic seizure onset on video-EEG. Clinical correlation recommended.
Abnormal values: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Total Learning) T-score 38 T-score (H), Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Delayed Recall) T-score 34 T-score (H).

findings.region: Left anterior and mesial temporal lobe
findings.signal: FDG_uptake: Significantly reduced FDG uptake compared to the contralateral right temporal lobe and to normative database; asymmetry_index: Left temporal metabolic asymmetry index -18% (significant, threshold > -10%)
FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake in frontal, parietal, occipital lobes and right temporal lobe

output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates significant left anterior and mesial temporal hypometabolism, concordant with left temporal lobe seizure onset on video-EEG and the subtle structural abnormality on 3T MRI. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: temporal lobe epilepsy association with deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism

results.title: Risk of venous thromboembolism in patients with epilepsy: a population-based cohort study
results.summary: Patients with epilepsy have a modestly increased risk of VTE compared to the general population (HR 1.4-1.8). Risk factors include prolonged post-ictal immobility, polypharmacy, and enzyme-inducing AEDs. Levetiracetam is not associated with increased VTE risk.

results.title: Seizures as a presenting feature of pulmonary embolism: a systematic review
results.summary: Seizures can rarely be the presenting or early feature of massive PE, likely through cerebral hypoxia or paradoxical embolism via patent foramen ovale. Reported in case reports and small series; most commonly generalized tonic-clonic seizures.

results.title: May-Thurner syndrome: diagnosis and management
results.summary: May-Thurner syndrome (iliac vein compression by the overlying right common iliac artery) is an underdiagnosed cause of left-sided DVT, particularly in young adults. Prevalence of anatomic compression in cadaveric studies is 22-32%, but clinically significant obstruction is much rarer. Associated with prolonged immobilization, oral contraceptives, and thrombophilia.

output.summary: Epilepsy and VTE can co-occur but are typically independent diagnoses. Seizures may rarely be a presenting feature of massive PE through cerebral hypoxia. The combination of left-sided DVT in a young person should raise suspicion for anatomic causes such as May-Thurner syndrome. In this patient, the temporal lobe epilepsy and the thromboembolic disease may represent two concurrent but pathophysiologically distinct diagnoses, or the PE could have contributed to seizure provocation through transient cerebral hypoxia.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam dose increase to 1500 mg BID with concurrent anticoagulation (heparin or enoxaparin followed by warfarin or DOAC)
interactions: No significant pharmacokinetic interactions between levetiracetam and direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivarelbandan, edoxaban)
No significant interactions between levetiracetam and heparin/enoxaparin
Levetiracetam is not hepatically metabolized and does not induce/inhibit CYP enzymes, making it a favorable AED choice in patients requiring anticoagulation
warnings: Monitor for levetiracetam-associated mood and behavioral side effects (irritability, depression), which may be exacerbated by stress of concurrent medical illness
If patient requires thrombolysis (tPA), there is a theoretical increased bleeding risk but levetiracetam itself does not affect coagulation
output.formulary_status: Levetiracetam and all listed anticoagulants are on formulary
alternatives: If levetiracetam is ineffective, lacosamide or lamotrigine are alternatives with minimal drug interaction potential for patients on anticoagulation
Avoid enzyme-inducing AEDs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) as they significantly reduce efficacy of DOACs through CYP3A4 induction

output.query: hypercoagulable workup young adult unprovoked DVT PE thrombophilia testing

results.title: Thrombophilia testing in venous thromboembolism: ISTH guidelines
results.summary: Routine thrombophilia testing is not recommended in unselected VTE patients. Testing may be considered in young patients (<50) with unprovoked VTE, strong family history, or unusual site thrombosis. Testing should not be performed during acute thrombosis or while on anticoagulation as results may be unreliable.

results.title: Trousseau syndrome: paraneoplastic VTE
results.summary: Paraneoplastic hypercoagulability (Trousseau syndrome) should be considered in patients with unprovoked VTE, especially with migratory thrombophlebitis or arterial events. Age-appropriate cancer screening recommended. Incidental lung nodules in VTE patients warrant further characterization.

output.summary: In a young patient with unprovoked VTE, thrombophilia testing (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A mutation, protein C, protein S, antithrombin III, antiphospholipid antibodies) should be considered but deferred until after acute treatment. The incidental lung nodule warrants dedicated chest CT to exclude malignancy as a potential prothrombotic driver (Trousseau syndrome). Anatomic compression (May-Thurner syndrome) should also be evaluated given the left-sided DVT.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (left temporal lobe epilepsy with focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures)

differential.key_distinguishing: PE-related syncope typically occurs during exertion or with sudden hemodynamic compromise, lacks post-ictal confusion, and EEG would not show temporal epileptiform discharges; however, PE was confirmed concurrently

differential.diagnosis: Provoked seizures from cerebral hypoxia secondary to pulmonary embolism
differential.key_distinguishing: Would expect seizures temporally linked to PE symptoms, but patient's first seizure preceded significant cardiopulmonary symptoms and EEG shows a clear focal temporal epileptogenic focus

differential.diagnosis: Cardiac arrhythmia with syncope (related to mitral valve prolapse)
differential.key_distinguishing: MVP rarely causes life-threatening arrhythmia; ECG and clinical course do not support primary cardiac etiology
Ruled out by video-EEG demonstrating clear electrographic seizure correlate with all clinical events

optimal_actions.action: Perform routine EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal routine EEG with benign variants (wicket spikes); does not exclude epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to evaluate for structural epileptogenic lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal standard MRI; incidental lung nodule noted on scout

optimal_actions.action: Obtain laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, coagulation panel, prolactin, and AED levels
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Markedly elevated D-dimer, elevated post-ictal prolactin, subtherapeutic levetiracetam level

optimal_actions.action: Order ECG to evaluate for cardiac arrhythmia or right heart strain
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with borderline tachycardia; no right heart strain pattern

optimal_actions.action: Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events and confirm epilepsy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left temporal epileptiform discharges and captured seizures with left temporal onset confirming focal epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Request 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol for subtle lesion detection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle left temporal pole abnormality suspicious for focal cortical dysplasia; borderline hippocampal volumetric asymmetry

optimal_actions.action: Optimize levetiracetam dose given subtherapeutic levels
Urgently evaluate for pulmonary embolism given elevated D-dimer, dyspnea, hemoptysis, and calf pain (separate from epilepsy workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: CTPA expected to show bilateral pulmonary emboli

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature to understand relationship between temporal lobe epilepsy, DVT, and pulmonary embolism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Epilepsy and VTE are typically independent; PE can rarely provoke seizures but focal temporal EEG findings indicate primary epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for levetiracetam with anticipated anticoagulation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant interactions; levetiracetam favorable in patients requiring anticoagulation

critical_actions: Recognize that normal routine EEG does not exclude epilepsy and pursue prolonged video-EEG monitoring
Identify the markedly elevated D-dimer and initiate urgent workup for pulmonary embolism (this is a life-threatening concurrent diagnosis)
Distinguish between primary focal epilepsy (temporal lobe) and provoked seizures from PE/hypoxia by noting the temporal sequence and EEG findings
Recognize the subtherapeutic levetiracetam level and recommend dose optimization
Identify the epigastric aura and deja vu episodes as focal aware seizures consistent with temporal lobe origin
Recognize that the lung nodule requires dedicated chest imaging to exclude malignancy (Trousseau syndrome consideration)
contraindicated_actions: Dismissing epilepsy based on a single normal routine EEG
Attributing all seizures to pulmonary embolism without confirming focal epileptogenic EEG findings
Prescribing enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (carbamazepine, phenytoin) that would interfere with anticoagulation therapy
Ignoring the elevated D-dimer and systemic symptoms (dyspnea, hemoptysis, calf pain) while focusing exclusively on the seizure workup
Performing lumbar puncture without first addressing the possible pulmonary embolism and anticoagulation needs
Discontinuing levetiracetam based on the normal routine EEG alone
key_reasoning_points: The patient presents with two concurrent but likely independent diagnoses: left temporal lobe epilepsy AND thromboembolic disease (DVT/PE), requiring dual diagnostic reasoning tracks

The initial normal EEG with wicket spikes (benign variant) is a diagnostic trap — wicket spikes in the temporal region may be misidentified as epileptiform, while the normal EEG may falsely reassure against epilepsy
The epigastric rising sensation and deja vu episodes represent unrecognized focal aware seizures (temporal lobe aura), establishing the focal onset of the generalized convulsions

Temporal sequence is key: the first seizure occurred BEFORE significant PE symptoms, suggesting primary epilepsy rather than PE-provoked seizures, though PE may have lowered seizure threshold subsequently
The incidental lung nodule creates a Trousseau syndrome red herring — while oncologic evaluation is warranted, it must not delay acute PE treatment or epilepsy diagnosis
Subtherapeutic levetiracetam level explains ongoing seizure breakthrough and is an immediately actionable finding
The early-morning timing of generalized seizures is a recognized pattern in temporal lobe epilepsy (seizures arising from NREM sleep)
Multi-modal concordance (video-EEG lateralization + 3T MRI subtle lesion + PET hypometabolism + neuropsychological verbal memory deficit) confirms left temporal lobe epilepsy

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle due to concurrent thromboembolic disease (DVT/PE) dominating the clinical presentation and creating misdirection from the epilepsy diagnosis. Normal initial EEG with benign variant wicket spikes, MRI-negative on standard sequences, incidental lung nodule raising Trousseau syndrome concern, and unrecognized focal aware seizures initially attributed to anxiety. The critical diagnostic clue (focal temporal epileptiform discharges and captured seizures) is only revealed on follow-up prolonged video-EEG.
